transcript.text: Hello. Here's another video request that I got seven hours ago. This one's from a Myers. Hello, Mr. Myers. Mr. Myers, Sarah. So, hi, Kyle. Video request. Can you please talk about your childhood? What was your favorite thing to do?
New, did you have a nickname? What was your favorite book show? Thank you and have a nice day. Okay. Please talk about my childhood.
I have a video series of like 18 or something, different videos where I talk, because I think that was when I could only do videos for 10 minutes, when I talk about my whole life.
And I start from when I'm born all the way up until like, um, the end of college. Because then after that, I had other video vlogs previously talking about traveling.
So I pretty much have my entire life all told out in detail. So it's a, it's a playlist. So if you're interested in that, you can watch that.
But I'll do this video just to put it in a synopsis really quickly. Um, I was born in Austin, um, December 27, 73. I was born the comet Kahootek. It's perihelion. It was a big comet.

This perihelion was on the 28th of December. But when I was born for like six hours, it was directly between the sun and the earth. And I was born when it was, when it was right between the sun and the earth and the sun.

Uh, that guy who killed himself with poison, with his little cult, whatever they were called. Uh, those guys thought that the Kahootek was a spaceship and there was a whole cult behind it. So that was pretty cool.
Uh, and then I lived originally in some town. Um, I don't know, some little duplex with a pool in the back in Austin, right next to the river.
But, and, but I don't remember any of that. And I think I was like, they said I was like three when I left. So for some reason, I don't remember it.
And I don't even remember it because I remember this nice house with a pool up on the, up on the top of the hill and, uh, Westlake Hills in Austin overlooking the downtown.
And, uh, we had like our own forest around us. It was badass. But I don't ever remember when I lived there having lived in another house.
Like we moved there. I think I vaguely remember it. Yeah. Now I'm starting to remember it, but yeah.
And that was fun, dude. I had an awesome childhood there because I was swimming back and forth in the pool from like as early as I could remember.
And it was a nice warm pool. And, uh, it was totally like, it was our own private pool. It was, we had a big, a big railing above the pool and a little big patio next to it with the big greenhouses.
And a little, uh, tent patio platform down the hill that we could go and camp out in.
And I was little, so I didn't even know like how far the forest went to me. It like went off forever. It was really cool.
And I went to some school, um, called, uh, Lamp and Lighter.
And I remember one time we went there for Christmas and my parents said that there, that Santa was on top of the roof of the school at the time, putting Christmas presents in.
And I remember believing them. I was like four.
And then I went to some school named, um, um, oh God, I can't remember it, but it was a big school.
I think it was a private school. And, uh, it was like multiple buildings. It was like a college campus.
And in one of the, the areas, there was a pool, but it was, uh, it was empty. So people, kids would plan it.
There was this broken down carcass plan. And then there was this dude with a go-kart who would take kids riding on the go-kart around this thing.
But there was a really long line to do it. So I only did it once.
But, uh, yeah. And then I was, and then when I was six, we moved to Boulder.
But I remember going to Colorado every summer for like three summers or something before that and knowing Boulder.
Because looking up at the Flatirons, before we even moved here, I mean, because there is the Buddhist, um, place, Rocky Mountain Dharma Center, RMDC.
I think it's called the Shambhala Center now. Up in the mountains outside of, um, Fort Collins.
But I thought that they were on the other side of the Flatirons when I was a little kid.
I looked at the Flatirons and they'd be like, yeah, it's in the mountains.
And they'd be like, oh, mountains? Like, behind the Flatirons. Oh, wow, they're behind the Flatirons.
But I didn't realize that you have to go north a couple hours and then go in the mountains.
Yeah. And then, uh, yeah, that was fun.
Because it was like thousands of people out there, like, Buddhist hippies smoking weed and drinking a shitload of alcohol.
And little kids drinking, too.
And just partying and doing the meditation and all that shit.
Burning the incense and throwing the rice and all that.
Playing the King of the Mountain and the, all the little, capture the flag and stuff.
Uh, yeah, so then we moved to Boulder when I was six because we, uh, we did, uh, they were Buddhists.
My parents were Buddhists. We went to a Buddhist grade school.
And then went to Waldorf, the private school.
And then went to Uni Hill private school right under the Flatirons.
And then Baseline Junior High and then Boulder High.
Growing up.
And, uh, when I was 10, my parents, there was this girl who was one day older than me who bought a, uh, who's, who for Christmas or for her birthday got her a 10-speed push, 10-speed bike.
And, uh, at that time I had a, uh, three-speed bike, banana seat.
And I was like, and I thought that was all badass.
So the other kids had BMX and I was riding it all over town on the bike paths all the time.
And, uh, I could go, like, wherever I want.
I was, like, the only kid who the kids' parents weren't, like, only go three blocks from here.
I got to go, like, all over.
It was cool.

Well, then I need a 10-speed.
I'm not letting some bitch have a 10-speed.
I only have my three-speed and I'm going to ride it all the time.

For the bike races.
